comment_text:
I just watched the documentary. I was sitting there, just before the end of the documentary, and I really immediately thought to myself: “Let me guess, there were no consequences at all.” Not even a minute later came the text just before the credits, which once again confirmed to me that it's always the same pattern:

1. something bad happens where people get hurt, die or something else bad, preferably something completely preventable

2. the bereaved and surviving victims are devastated and long for justice

3.. Those responsible go to court and are then completely acquitted without any consequences. Ideally, they will be even more successful at what they do than ever before

4. a documentary is released in which the bereaved and surviving victims say again that they should be heard, that something like this should never happen again, etc.

5. since those responsible got away with everything without any problems, nothing has been learned and it will happen again, while the victims and survivors will suffer for the rest of their lives without receiving any kind of compensation or even sympathy and compassion from the public.

And then these five points are repeated over and over and over again. Because a gigantic corporation like Live Nation or stars like Traivs Scott use their wealth to buy the best lawyers and thus simply buy justice. After all, money rules the world and always wins in the end. Or in classic criminal cases, the reason is simply the justice system itself repeatedly suffers an absolute total collapse. I've watched a lot of documentaries about tragic events or criminal cases and it just tires me out.

comment_text:
People defending Travis Scott are complete fools. Even if he didn’t know what was going on, Live Nation did. If you’re too much of a Travis D**k rider to watch the documentary before you jump in and defend him - Live nation team members were texting saying “there’s CPR going on, in my 25 years of doing this I’ve never seen so many bodies being passed over the rail, someone is going to end up dying” and they ignored it.

Travis then continued working with that same company to tour the world making record amounts of cash. LMeanwhile those families go and visit the tombstones of their dead relatives and whose lives will be forever ruined. Imagine, you put on a show and 10 people die, and you put out an apology video and don’t have a single tear in your eye? 

God damn, I’d be balling my eyes out. And certainly saying - I’ll never work with this company until they take accountability. And does Travis post on the anniversary of the event remembering those fans? No. He’s part of sweeping it all under the rug.

A grim situation all around. RIP to those beautiful souls who past, and those who are still with us but have to live with the memories of that night. Travis has no balls or morals, or else he would have stood up and spoke out against live nation.

comment_text:
Les responsables sont les organisateurs et Travis Scott. Je travaille dans l'événementiel et je n'ai jamais vu un dispositif de sécurité comme celui là, et la capacité totale était bien trop faible déjà pour 50k personnes et plus les entrées sans tickets, c'est inconcevable. Une pensée pour les victimes. Comment peut-on laisser continuer se dérouler un concert alors que des massages cardiaques sont en cours et que des centaines de personnes sont évacués à cause de malaise. Dès le premier massage cardiaque ou si la sécurité est débordé à cause de multiples malaise, le concert doit s'arrêter définitivement, sa veut dire qu'il y a de gros problème. Honteux... Et aussi à ceux qui disent, ils n'avaient pas qu'à courir ou à rentrer sans billet, oui certainement. Mais c'est aussi au rôle de la sécurité et des responsables de l'événement, de prévoir tout les options et d'empêcher que des débordements commence des l'entrée, dans une salle de concert ou un festival, la sécurité et tout le staff sont responsables des spectateurs et visiteurs. Faut se demander pourquoi ils ont pû rentrer si facilement ?! Pourquoi faire rentrer tout le monde d'un seul coup et non pas par vague ?!
Le gros problème dans se genre de festival, comme Woodstock, c'est l'organisation, la sécurité, qui ne savent pas dire stop, qui ne prévoit pas assez, et qui veulent toujours toujours plus de rentabilité alors ils surcharge le nombre de spectateurs. Si les organisateurs ne mettent pas de règles dans se genre de festival, ou de bonnes mesures de sécurité, ça déborde forcément...

comment_text: I watched it this morning...honestly...one thing that really stood out to me was how overwhelmed the police and emergency responders were once the crowd surge began. It’s clear this was a failure of systems (not one person). Watching how slow the chain of command moved, it’s heartbreaking to think how many lives might’ve been saved if law enforcement had been given real authority over the event’s crowd planning and emergency shutdown protocols. This wasn’t about blaming an artist or fans; it was about a lack of structural control when it was most needed. My prayers are with the families, and I hope this becomes a turning point for how mass events are handled going forward. 🖤🕊

video_title: Trainwreck: The Astroworld Tragedy | Official Trailer | Netflix
comment_text:
I wanna get my story out. I remember being at the concert as soon as stargazing started to play I remember about eight people in my section started to fall so I already picked up 4 people from the ground  when the first song started to play, 
  My brother was right next to me. He picked up 3 people from the ground and I remember in the song stargazing the second verse a huge beat drops and this kid fell down and I got this feeling like if I don’t pick him up, he could get stomped on pretty badly, as soon as I pick him up off the ground, the beat drops and I’m carrying his body with me and we’re bouncing up and down. My brother still next to me during this part ,we try to hold hands to stay close. We got a hard grip we look at each other ,and jokingly we’re telling each other,” I just saved another life!” 
  I picked 5 people up from the ground that night from the first song , my brother picked up 3 , 

crazy because saving lives is what we actually did and in the moment we didn’t realize the extent of what just happened. 
 
It was so tight, Very hard to move and the crowd eventually separated my brother and I , there was no shot we could stick together, I remember, trying to navigate a way out of the crowd and I couldn’t believe how never ending the crowd was , it was bigger than I knew . 

We were all safe and were shocked when the concert was over as soon as we got to the hotel it was already reported that people died .
 
It was a unique time to be at a concert. Covid had just happened. We hadn’t been at a show in over a year, going on nearly 2 years. There were a lot of foreshadowing going on during the prior concerts like Don Toliver , women couldn’t breathe and things like that , but because of the uniqueness of not being at a show in so long, we were having trouble acknowledging like people not breathing well, was that OK? Was it not? We were all unsure because nobody was expecting death , we all came to Astro world to have the greatest time , ever! 
Nobody was hurt at those Don Toliver concerts , but it, was raising some red flags and questions, and foreshadowing to what was to come that night.

video_title: Trainwreck: The Astroworld Tragedy | Official Trailer | Netflix
comment_text:
Why Travis Scott Was Not Responsible for the Astroworld Tragedy 

The Astroworld Festival tragedy of November 2021 was a devastating event that resulted in the deaths of ten concertgoers and injuries to hundreds more. While Travis Scott, the festival’s headlining artist, faced intense scrutiny and blame, the Netflix documentary *Trainwreck: The Astroworld Tragedy* and subsequent investigations provide compelling evidence that he was not responsible for the disaster. Several key arguments support this conclusion. 

1. Travis Scott Did Not Have the Authority 
to Stop the Show 

One of the most significant revelations from the documentary is that only **two individuals** had the authority to stop the concert. Contrary to popular belief, Travis Scott was not one of them. While artists can sometimes pause performances if they notice disturbances, the ultimate decision to halt the event rested with festival officials and security personnel. Scott was wearing in-ear monitors, which are designed to relay important instructions, but reports indicate that he was only told to stop performing when a guest artist was about to come on stage—not because of the unfolding tragedy. 

2. Lack of Communication and Information 

Scott has repeatedly stated in interviews that he was unaware of the severity of the situation while performing. The documentary supports this claim, showing that critical information about the crowd crush was not relayed to him. A crowd safety expert featured in the film revealed that an audio engineer was warned about multiple active CPR cases and possible fatalities, but this information was not passed on to Scott. Without real-time updates, he had no way of knowing the extent of the crisis. 

3. The Festival’s Poor Planning and 
Security Failures 

The documentary highlights several organizational failures that contributed to the disaster. Live Nation, the event’s promoter, reportedly sold **50,000 tickets** despite concerns that the venue could not safely accommodate that number. Security personnel struggled to control the crowd from the moment the gates opened, with attendees rushing past barriers and overwhelming staff. Experts interviewed in the film argue that the tragedy was **not an accident but an inevitability** due to a lack of foresight and basic safety protocols. 

4. The History of Chaotic Concerts and Misplaced Blame 

Travis Scott’s concerts have long been known for their high-energy atmosphere, but blaming him for the tragedy ignores the systemic failures that led to the crowd crush. The documentary includes footage of concertgoers attempting to alert a cameraman about the unfolding crisis, but their warnings were ignored. Additionally, past incidents at other festivals have shown that crowd surges can happen regardless of an artist’s actions. The film suggests that the chaos was **predictable** and should have been mitigated by event organizers. 

5. The Grand Jury’s Decision and Legal Outcomes 

In 2023, a grand jury declined to indict Travis Scott, Live Nation, or other organizers on criminal charges, reinforcing the argument that no single individual was solely responsible for the tragedy. In 2024, Scott and Live Nation settled wrongful death lawsuits, further indicating that the blame was shared among multiple parties rather than resting solely on the artist. 

6. Scott’s Reaction and Emotional Response 

Following the tragedy, Scott expressed deep sorrow and pledged to support victims’ families. In interviews, he has maintained that he did not hear or see the distress in the crowd. The documentary provides insight into his reaction during the performance, particularly during the song *90210*, where he briefly paused after noticing someone unconscious. While some believed he continued singing to calm the crowd, the film suggests that he was actually **confused and unsure of what to do**, as no instructions were given to him. 

Conclusion 

The Astroworld tragedy was a result of **multiple failures**, including poor event planning, inadequate security measures, and a lack of communication between festival staff and performers. While Travis Scott was the face of the event, the documentary and legal investigations make it clear that he was **not responsible** for the disaster. The blame lies primarily with the organizers who failed to ensure the safety of attendees. As more people watch the documentary and understand the full context, the hope is that public perception will shift, recognizing that Scott was a performer caught in a tragic situation rather than the cause of it.

comment_text:
The Gospel represented in the Crucifixion

At the crucifixion, we see Jesus hanging between two thieves. The two thieves represent all of humanity: sinners who stand before a Savior, each faced with a choice. One mocked and rejected Christ. The other recognized Him as Lord and, in that moment of faith, was promised, “Today you will be with Me in Paradise.”

This powerful image reminds us that we, too, are given the freedom to choose. We can either accept the gift of salvation offered through Jesus’ sacrifice—or we can reject it. But make no mistake: the choice carries eternal weight.

Romans 6:23 tells us, “For the wages of sin is death; but the gift of God is eternal life through Jesus Christ our Lord.”
If we accept Christ, we are saved, sealed, and assured a place with Him in eternity. If we turn away, we face the just penalty for sin.

So when you reflect on the two thieves at Calvary, remember: you are not just a spectator—you are one of them. The question Jesus asked His disciples still echoes through time:

“But whom say ye that I am?” — Matthew 16:15 (KJV)

Now who do you say he is?
Your answer is everything.
